hard.next_step: June 29th - Commerical Discussion w/o July 20th - Likeley on-site in Boston for deep-dive trial Internal review of approch and pricing for what amounts to be an MSP model Could be common instance with isolation or could be multiple individual instances if latter than direct billing with scaling rebate - though may be better to front these with RA/Northestern Suggested Next Steps from Roy Anderson - Will control this so as not to over exercise without seeing outcomes. Next steps is to discuss pricing based on the multiple scenarios. Crawl with NU and up to $100M in spend over 18 months Walk with 2 other schools, multiple input and output engines Run in full production, 20+ schools Mind blown with 100 schools in production Time frame: week of June 8 th or 15th. I would anticipate that this will be an initial high-level discussion at first and create general concepts of total costs going forward. More detailed review and contractual discussion when NU signs up for the program. Second is a more hands on, in-depth, review of the tool set We will need to see it in action A site visit of one of your customers Live action with one student as buyer, two as sellers. Walk through the end-to-end process, in a live environment using NU spend data, and select product, put it out to bid. What We Want to See the AI Agent Do: Ingest context from a brief plain-language description of the need (typed or spoken) and generate a structured, category-appropriate RFP draft Automatically populate standard sections: scope of work, technical requirements, service levels, pricing model structure, evaluation criteria, and terms/conditions framework Reference historical category data and market benchmarks (e.g., Staffing Industry Analysts, Bureau of Labor Statistics rates) to inform rate card structure Suggest a recommended supplier shortlist based on category fit, past performance indicators, and diversity/ESG criteria Identify missing information and prompt the user to complete gaps — do not silently skip required fields Allow iterative refinement via conversational AI ("change the pricing structure to time-and-materials only" or "add a cybersecurity-specific SLA section") The goal is to see if the following can be shown Dimension What We Are Evaluating 1. Practical Utility Does the AI agent produce outputs that a seasoned procurement professional would actually use without significant rework? Is the quality of the RFP or negotiation strategy at a CPO-ready standard? 2. Reasoning Transparency Can the evaluator understand why the agent made each decision? Is the AI's logic explainable, auditable, and defensible to a CFO or Board? Hidden black-box logic is disqualifying. 3. Human-AI Teaming Is the handoff between AI action and human decision well-designed? Does the system know what it doesn't know? Are escalation triggers meaningful and configurable? 4. Integration Depth Does the AI agent leverage real organizational data (spend history, supplier master, contract terms) or operate in a generic vacuum? Can it connect to ERPs, market data feeds, or external databases? 5. Risk & Compliance Design Does the system enforce approval thresholds, flag non-compliant terms, maintain audit trails, and respect data privacy requirements? Is it enterprise-grade in its governance architecture? Demonstration of Spend and ANA set for 26th may
hard.ais_why:
• Northeastern has an 'entrepreneurial bent to create a re-usable, scalable model combining spend visibility and tactical tail spend agents' ‚Äî 'tail spend agents' is an explicit agentic concept.
• ANA and Merlin are scoped, aligning with the autonomous tail-spend vision the program described.
• The initiative is still concept-stage (Boston round-table origin, 1:1 follow-up) and aims to leverage a quick win outward, so the strong idea is not yet backed by a committed evaluation.
hard.primary_competitor: Multi-vendor MSP fragmentation (Focal Point, Fair Market)

north_star_verdict.headline: Live, weekly-cadence co-development deal driven hard by Northeastern lecturer Roy Anderson, but a 30 Sep close is not credible: still Qualified and concept-stage, the pilot itself only starts in September, pricing is mid-iteration, and no economic buyer has validated the 2% supplier-funded model.
north_star_verdict.math: Today 2026-07-06. Close 2026-09-30 = 86 days out. Created and Qualified 2026-05-20, so ~47 days in Qualified. Last buyer call 29 Jun (7 days ago); incoming buyer email 26 Jun; tasks through 02-09 Jul with an on-site hands-on trial being locked for the week of 20 Jul and an implementation discussion set for 8-10 Jul. Engagement is strong and current. But the deal is still Qualified: the commercial framework is mid-iteration, the pilot ($5-10M) only starts in September, single-vs-multi-tenant feasibility is unresolved, and legal/implementation are open. The remaining steps cannot complete by 30 Sep on current pace, so the date will slip even though the deal is healthy.
evidence: Roy Anderson called the pricing framework 'really good' on the 29 Jun commercial call and moved to lock an on-site hands-on trial for the week of 20 Jul (source: Avoma call 29 Jun).

The September pilot ($5-10M crawl on NU spend) is only scoped, not launched: 'why don't we start it with a 5,000,000 or 10,000,000 program, and let's prove that this works' (source: Avoma call 09 Jun).

No Northeastern economic buyer has validated the 2% supplier-funded revenue model; funding is 'from Northeastern University initially' but unconfirmed by any NU finance owner (source: Avoma call 29 Jun).
The internal alignment meeting Roy referenced ('Tish' meeting, week of 8-15 Jun) was never confirmed back to the deal team (source: overdue SF task 15 Jun).

metrics.narrative: The business problem is a scalable, monetizable university procurement program: Roy Anderson wants to run a student-powered managed-service model across 20 and eventually 50-100 schools, funded by a 2% supplier success fee, starting with Northeastern's roughly $100M of addressable spend ('there's a 100,000,000 that you think is addressable', 09 Jun). Arthur quantified the macro pain in the 26 May demo using Hackett data: a 9.8% rise in procurement workload against near-flat staffing, driving the need for agentic tail-spend automation. What is missing is a validated NU savings/ROI number: Roy is explicitly forward-looking ('yesterday is irrelevant... I wanna go with clean data going forward', 29 Jun), so no historical-baseline savings case has been built. Quantifying the pilot's expected savings and the program's supplier-fee economics for a Northeastern budget owner is the open move.

economic_buyer.narrative: No verified economic buyer has engaged. The commercial framework Arthur sent on 29 Jun states funding 'comes from Northeastern University initially', and Roy positions himself as the program's controller of pace and of the 2% supplier revenue ('Will control this so as not to over exercise', SF Next Step). Roy champions the program upward to a senior sponsor he refers to only as 'Tish' ('that was with the tish... I told you I would champion it... I can't get any higher than him', 09 Jun), but that person has no Salesforce contact, carries no confirmed title, and has never appeared on a call, so budget authority is unvalidated. No CFO, CPO, or procurement executive has been reached. The move is to get Roy to bring the internal sponsor and a named NU finance owner onto a call to validate the supplier-funded model.
sources: SF task 'Confirm outcome of internal alignment meeting' 15 Jun

decision_criteria.narrative: Roy documented an explicit five-dimension AI-agent evaluation rubric in his quasi-RFP (SF Next Step): Practical Utility (CPO-ready RFP output a seasoned pro would use without rework), Reasoning Transparency ('Hidden black-box logic is disqualifying'), Human-AI Teaming (meaningful, configurable escalation triggers), Integration Depth (leverages real spend history, supplier master and contract terms, connects to ERPs and market data feeds), and Risk & Compliance Design (approval thresholds, audit trails, data privacy). Alongside these functional criteria sit hard commercial criteria surfaced on 29 Jun: single-tenant-per-university versus a shared-taxonomy cube, per-university license scaling (110k/104k/88k), minimized implementation cost, and supplier-master handling without a mandatory supplier portal. These are confirmed and specific, giving Zycus a clear scoring target for the 20 Jul trial.

decision_process.narrative: Roy is running an informal, founder-led process and explicitly rejects a formal RFP ('I'd rather just call it coming to a decision rather than anything formal', 09 Jun), controlling cadence himself. The sequence is: iterate the commercial framework by email, run an on-site hands-on trial the week of 20 Jul with Zycus solution consultant Devika, then start a September pilot ($5-10M) on real NU spend, with a broader contract to follow success. Internally he socializes to the 'Tish' sponsor and is recruiting ~10 students as future operators. The approval and procurement chain beyond Roy is undefined, and the internal alignment meeting slated for the week of 8-15 Jun was never confirmed. Mapping who inside Northeastern must approve a multi-year, multi-school commitment is the gap to close.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting has not started. Arthur sent a 'commercial framework' on 29 Jun focused on a single-tenant-per-university model, but there is no MSA, Order Form, or SOW in draft; a formal contract is explicitly deferred until after the September pilot proves out ('more detailed review and contractual discussion when NU signs up for the program', SF Next Step). The only live legal threads are minor and program-specific: Roy asked Zycus to clear students citing and publishing Zycus in research papers, and asked to be added to a referral/co-marketing agreement (29 Jun). Given this is a novel MSP/GPO structure, visibility would come from agreeing the pilot scope in writing and getting Northeastern procurement/legal identified before the pilot, so contracting does not stall on an undefined paper path.
sources: SF task 'Check with legal... case studies' 09 Jun

identify_pain.narrative: Confirmed pain sits on two levels. Institutionally, the incumbents are failing: Roy said Jaggaer 'is going to literally fall, die on the vine because no one wants to use it because it's so hard to use', and Workday 'is not working very well for them... it really requires a whole reboot' (07 May), while tail spend goes unmanaged and procurement workload outpaces staffing (26 May demo). Personally and strategically, Roy's pain is the absence of a scalable, monetizable, student-run university procurement model and a lack of an AI agent that produces CPO-ready, auditable output. The pain owner is Roy Anderson as program originator, with downstream owners being Northeastern's procurement staff who cannot adopt the current tools. This is real and articulated, not inferred.

champion.narrative: Roy Anderson (College Lecturer, D'Amore-McKim School of Business) is a clearly identified and highly active champion: he originated the program, drives the agenda and the weekly cadence, pulls the next steps ('I'd love to have you... the implementation person', 29 Jun), is recruiting ~10 students, is offering three years of cleaned NU spend data and a co-op partnership, and claims internal backing ('I've got executive backing on it as well', 09 Jun). His advocacy is genuine and he is the deal's engine. The strength is rated developing rather than strong because he is a lecturer whose budget authority is unproven, the sponsor he escalates to ('Tish') has never engaged, and he is the sole thread: no Northeastern procurement or finance stakeholder has been reached. What has developed is deep commercial co-design; what is still missing is proof that Roy can convert institutional approval and budget.

competition.narrative: This is a greenfield co-development play, not a head-to-head bake-off, so the strongest current threat is multi-vendor fragmentation rather than a named rival winning. Roy intends a multi-vendor MSP and is protecting standing relationships: he wants to keep Focal Point's Anders Liljevic ('Anders is like, I'm in, I want to be a part of this', 09 Jun) and Fair Market for auctions ('we may have to work with focal point', 29 Jun), and when asked directly whether he would choose one platform or 'play the field' he said flexibility is inevitable. Jaggaer and Workday are failing incumbents being displaced, not defended, and Pactam is only an AI-negotiation benchmark. The wedge is to make Zycus the single RA platform standard (spend + iSource + ANA + Merlin) proven at the 20 Jul trial and September pilot, framing Focal Point as an orchestration add-on rather than a parallel core vendor, before rivals secure parallel scopes.

deal_movement.summary: Engagement is live and strengthening: a pricing framework was delivered on 29 Jun and is iterating, on-site trial dates for the week of 20 Jul and an 8-10 Jul implementation discussion are being locked, and the last two-way buyer touch was Roy's 29 Jun call plus his 26 Jun email. The close date was pushed 30 Jul to 30 Sep on 09 Jun.

items.change: Arthur presented and began iterating the commercial framework ($30k license + $50k implementation for the first $100M spend band; per-university licenses 110k/104k/88k; 2% supplier success fee model)
Zycus emailed the commercial framework and its position on students citing Zycus in research to Roy
On-site hands-on trial targeted for the week of 20 Jul and implementation discussion set for 8-10 Jul with Hemant and Devika
26 May demo of Spend + ANA to Roy and student Pranay drove additional interest

competitive_position.summary: Greenfield co-development, not a live bake-off. The strongest CURRENT threat (2026) is multi-vendor MSP fragmentation: Roy intends a multi-vendor program and wants to retain Focal Point (Anders Liljevic, orchestration, buyer-preferred partner) and Fair Market (auctions), so Zycus risks being one tool among several rather than the platform standard (09 and 29 Jun). Jaggaer is the failing incumbent sourcing system being displaced and Workday the failing upstream incumbent (07 May); Pactam is an AI-negotiation benchmark only. Do-nothing/internal-stall is a moderate inertia risk given the program is concept-stage. Win by making Zycus the single RA platform standard proven at the 20 Jul trial and September pilot.

competitors.name: Multi-vendor MSP fragmentation
competitors.quote: it is my anticipation to to have one solution, but let's be honest, the universities are gonna force some level of flexibility
competitors.how_we_win: Make Zycus the single RA platform standard (spend + iSource + ANA + Merlin) proven at the 20 Jul trial; show the integration cost and governance gaps of stitching multiple point tools.

competitors.name: Fair Market
competitors.quote: your example of fair market is a great example of someone where you could buy 6 auctions from
competitors.how_we_win: Differentiate full-suite AI autonomous sourcing (ANA) versus point auction capability.

competitors.name: Jaggaer (incumbent sourcing, via iQuest/Cyquest)
competitors.quote: they do have Jagger, it's not working that well... Jagger is going to literally fall, die on the vine because no one wants to use it because it's so hard to use
competitors.how_we_win: Lean into poor adoption; deliver an easy, low-touch flow students can run and prove autonomous sourcing on real NU tail spend at the July 20 trial.

competitors.name: Workday (incumbent upstream procurement)
competitors.quote: They implemented Workday, which is not working very well for them... it really requires a whole reboot
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus as the MSP layer that flips awards back into Workday for invoicing via flat-file exchange, avoiding a rip-and-replace fight.

competitors.name: Pactam
competitors.quote: find out where are you on your AI journey... benchmark against PACTAM performance
competitors.how_we_win: Treat as an AI-negotiation benchmark; prove Merlin/ANA reasoning transparency against Roy's five criteria at the trial.

competitors.name: Do Nothing / internal stall
competitors.quote: Northeastern has been really slow... I am not hard pedaling it yet
competitors.how_we_win: Use the supplier-funded model (no real cost to schools) and a fast, low-risk September pilot to remove the inertia and concept-stage barrier.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong fit on Roy's functional AI-agent criteria and on the low-touch integration model; the exposed areas are proving reasoning transparency at the trial and validating the novel 2% supplier-funded commercial model with a budget owner.

items.quote: No Northeastern CFO, CPO or budget owner has been on any call; funding stated as 'from Northeastern University initially' with the 'Tish' sponsor unverified and never engaged
Roy Anderson is the only Salesforce contact; the sole other buyer-side face is a student (Pranay); no procurement staff mapped

items.area: Internal Northeastern mandate/approval for the program unproven
items.quote: The program is concept-stage (Boston round-table origin) and Roy controls pace 'so as not to over exercise'; no institutional approval forum confirmed
Real competition (Focal Point, Fair Market, incumbent Jaggaer/Workday) lives in the 07/09/29 Jun calls, not the field

best_practice_check.summary: Deal is genuinely live but early, single-threaded on Roy, and at risk of vendor fragmentation. The three highest-impact levers are multi-threading to a Northeastern budget owner, converting the 20 Jul trial into a scored proof, and locking Zycus as the named core platform.
flags: Multi-thread beyond Roy: get a Northeastern procurement or finance owner onto a call to validate the 2% supplier-funded model and budget before the September pilot.
Turn the 20 Jul on-site trial into a scored run against Roy's five AI-agent criteria (especially reasoning transparency and integration depth) using real NU spend data.
Get Zycus named as the single core RA platform in Roy's quasi-RFP, framing Focal Point as an orchestration add-on rather than a parallel vendor.

items.name: Roy Anderson
items.title: College Lecturer
items.risk: Single-threaded; budget authority unproven; his internal sponsor ('Tish') has never engaged

champion_strength.summary: Roy Anderson is a genuine, highly active champion and the deal's engine, but he is a lecturer with unproven budget authority, single-threaded, and his internal sponsor is unengaged, so his advocacy has not yet been converted into institutional approval.
champion_strength.source: SF OpportunityContactRole; Avoma calls 09 Jun, 29 Jun

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI is the centerpiece and it is resonating: Roy is evaluating agentic RFP generation and autonomous negotiation, probed the foundational model (Zycus is on Claude/Anthropic private instance per 26 May), and set 'Hidden black-box logic is disqualifying' as a bar. Zycus is well positioned but must prove reasoning transparency and CPO-ready output at the trial.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS reads 6.0 / Moderate, but the calls show stronger AI appetite than moderate: Roy's entire thesis is agentic tail-spend procurement, his students write agentic AI programs, and he interrogated the foundational-model stack. The field likely under-rates this deal's AI hunger.

items.detail: No Northeastern economic buyer, CFO or CPO engaged; Roy is the sole thread and the 'Tish' sponsor has never joined a call
items.source: Avoma calls 09/29 Jun; overdue SF task 15 Jun

items.detail: Multi-vendor fragmentation: Roy's personal loyalty to the relevant stakeholder (Anders Liljevic) and Fair Market could keep Zycus from becoming the standard
The 2% supplier-funded revenue model is unvalidated by any NU finance owner; funding 'from Northeastern University initially' is unconfirmed
A 30 Sep close is not credible: the pilot itself only starts in September and pricing/feasibility/legal are all open

items.detail: Concept-stage, entrepreneurial program dependent on student labor and internal buy-in; risk of internal stall if the sponsor deprioritizes it

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is live and current, not cooling: last buyer call 29 Jun, buyer email 26 Jun, tasks through 02-09 Jul, and an on-site trial being locked for the week of 20 Jul. Roy is pulling next steps rather than being pushed.
items: Weekly commercial cadence held from early May through 29 Jun
Buyer requested the implementation-lead follow-up and named the July 20 on-site window himself
Buyer email received 26 Jun offering availability for business-terms discussion

items.action: Run the 8-10 Jul implementation session with Hemant and Devika to resolve single-tenant vs shared-cube feasibility.
items.trigger: Roy asked for an implementation-lead follow-up to reduce implementation anxiety; dates being set
items.expected_effect: Unblocks the pricing model and firms the 20 Jul on-site, keeping the September pilot on track.

items.action: Send Roy the revised pricing Excel with the $100M base plus $500M/$1B and per-school 1-20 scale scenarios.
items.trigger: Roy asked for full pricing and scale scenarios; the revised-math task is overdue
items.expected_effect: Gives Roy the commercial basis to socialize internally and advance toward a pilot agreement.

items.action: Design the 20 Jul on-site trial as a scored run against Roy's five AI-agent criteria using real NU spend data.
items.trigger: Roy documented an explicit five-dimension evaluation rubric and wants a live end-to-end run
items.expected_effect: Proves reasoning transparency and integration depth, locking Zycus as the platform standard over Focal Point/Fair Market.

items.action: Ask Roy to bring his internal sponsor and a Northeastern finance owner onto a call to validate the 2% supplier-funded model.

items.expected_effect: Clears two open buyer asks that build Roy's trust and cement his champion role.

critical_signals.text: Roy wants a multi-vendor program and is protecting his Focal Point and Fair Market relationships, so Zycus is not yet the named single platform standard.
The 29 Jun commercial call landed well: Roy called the pricing framework really good and moved to lock an on-site hands-on trial for the week of 20 Jul.
The only new buyer-side face is a student, Pranay, the incoming supply-chain-club VP; no Northeastern procurement executive or budget owner has been engaged.
Pricing is framework-stage (roughly $30k license plus $50k implementation for the first $100M band, funded by a 2% supplier success fee) and no budget owner has validated it.
Roy newly asked Zycus legal to clear students citing and publishing Zycus in research and to add him to the referral/co-marketing program.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: Roy Anderson, a Northeastern lecturer, is co-developing a student-run university procurement GPO with Zycus and is racing us toward a September pilot; the deal is genuinely live but still concept-stage, single-threaded on Roy, with no engaged economic buyer, and he intends to keep Focal Point and other vendors in the mix rather than name Zycus the sole platform.

win_position.text: We sit first for the platform core: Roy asked Zycus for a full commercial model, an on-site hands-on trial the week of 20 Jul, self-serve links for his students, and co-op access, and called the pricing framework 'really good' (29 Jun), but the program is still concept-stage at Qualified with no signed commercial.

Roy intends a multi-vendor MSP and wants to keep Focal Point's Anders Liljevic and Fair Market in the program ('it certainly could be play', 09 Jun), so Zycus risks being one tool among several rather than the standard.
The buyer is a single lecturer: no Northeastern procurement executive, CFO or CPO has been reached, and the sponsor Roy champions up to ('Tish') has never joined a call through 02 Jul.

deal_momentum.text: Weekly cadence held through 29 Jun with a pricing framework delivered and iterating, an on-site trial being locked for the week of 20 Jul, and an implementation discussion set for 8-10 Jul.
Roy is pulling, not being pushed: he requested the implementation-lead follow-up and named the July 20 window himself (29 Jun).
Movement is all rep-and-Roy: no new decision stakeholder has joined and the internal alignment ('Tish') meeting slated for the week of 8-15 Jun was never confirmed (overdue 15 Jun).

customer_commitment.text: Roy committed real assets: three years of cleaned NU spend data, up to $100M addressable spend, a September pilot ($5-10M), and ~10 students he is recruiting for the sandbox (29 Jun).

No economic buyer has validated the 2% supplier-funded revenue model and no budget or contract is committed; funding is 'from Northeastern University initially' but unconfirmed by any NU finance owner (29 Jun).

deal_risk.text: Single-threaded on one lecturer with an entrepreneurial, concept-stage program that could stall internally if the 'Tish' sponsor deprioritizes it (09 Jun).

A 30 Sep close is not credible: the pilot itself only starts in September, pricing is mid-iteration, and legal, implementation feasibility and the single-vs-multi-tenant model are all open (09/29 Jun).

multi_threading.evidence: Single-threaded on Roy Anderson; only other buyer-side face is a student; no NU procurement exec engaged through 02 Jul.

economic_buyer_engaged.evidence: No verified EB; the 2% supplier-funded model is unvalidated and the 'Tish' sponsor has never joined a call (29 Jun).

competitor_preferred.evidence: Roy wants to retain Focal Point and Fair Market; Zycus not yet named the single standard (09 Jun).

scope_change.from: Phased crawl/walk/run university GPO (NU $100M, then 2-5 then 20+ schools)
scope_change.to: Same phased plan; September pilot ($5-10M) as the crawl step
scope_change.detail: The phasing is by design (Roy's crawl/walk/run), not a defensive shrink; amount ($250k) and product scope (ML iAnalyze, Merlin, ANA) are unchanged versus the prior record.

expansion_context.note: Net-new logo in Education; no prior Closed-Won on Northeastern, so no inherited executive access. Multi-threading to a Northeastern procurement/finance owner is genuinely open and is a real gap, not a suppressed one.

customer_preference.evidence: Roy is Zycus-specific and enthusiastic: asked for a commercial proposal, an on-site hands-on trial, student self-serve links and co-op partnership, and called the pricing framework 'really good' (29 Jun). Tempered by his stated intent to keep Focal Point and Fair Market in a multi-vendor program.

business_case.evidence: Qualitative model is clear (2% supplier success fee funds a scalable student-run GPO on ~$100M NU addressable spend), but no quantified NU savings/ROI has been validated by a finance owner.

contributions.evidence: customer requested next meeting (from call evidence).
Verdict flags close-date risk this sweep.
Next Step carries 1 dated milestone(s) — actively worked.

commentary.win_position: Win 23 is carried by momentum 63 vs stage-expected 50; preference strength +1.00 (weight 20). Offsetting it: competitive strength -1.00 (weight 15).
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 63 (moving forward): commercial topics entering (from call evidence). Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment sits near the earned-from-zero floor: little observable customer investment surfaced. Commitment is earned, so a low value is honest, not a penalty.
commentary.deal_risk: Risk 0: no break-risk was observed in the swept evidence. Coverage is thin, so read this as 'nothing seen yet,' not 'nothing there.'
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 16 is anchored on the stage win (23), nudged by commitment 8 and momentum 63, and dragged by risk 0.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Partial Read: 5 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (stage integrity, competitive, exec and champion, commercial, forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.

cro_panel.intro: Roy Anderson, a Northeastern lecturer, is co-developing a student-run university procurement GPO with Zycus and is racing us toward a September pilot; the deal is genuinely live but still concept-stage, single-threaded on Roy, with no engaged economic buyer, and he intends to keep Focal Point and other vendors in the mix rather than name Zycus the sole platform

A 30 Sep close is not credible; the pilot itself only starts in September, pricing is mid-iteration, and legal, implementation feasibility and the single-vs-multi-tenant model are all open (09/29 Jun)

blocks.footer: Competitive picture: Greenfield co-development, not a live bake-off.

blocks.title: Customer commitment
blocks.read: Light — they haven't committed much yet.

bullets.text: Roy committed real assets; three years of cleaned NU spend data, up to $100M addressable spend, a September pilot ($5-10M), and ~10 students he is recruiting for the sandbox (29 Jun)

No economic buyer has validated the 2% supplier-funded revenue model and no budget or contract is committed; funding is 'from Northeastern University initially' but unconfirmed by any NU finance owner (29 Jun)

blocks.title: What moves it forward
items: Run the 8-10 Jul implementation session with Hemant and Devika to resolve single-tenant vs shared-cube feasibility. (by 10 Jul)
Send Roy the revised pricing Excel with the $100M base plus $500M/$1B and per-school 1-20 scale scenarios. (by 15 Jul)
Design the 20 Jul on-site trial as a scored run against Roy's five AI-agent criteria using real NU spend data. (by 20 Jul)

gaps: No Northeastern economic buyer, CFO, CPO or procurement executive engaged on any call (through 02 Jul).
Single-threaded: Roy Anderson is the only Salesforce contact; only other buyer face is a student.
Internal Northeastern program mandate/approval process unconfirmed.
validation gate: a named person in a to-do/action item is in no Salesforce contact role, Avoma attendee list, or active-user roster and is anchored to no source (offending: 'Focal Point')

validation gate: an active competitor is named in no Salesforce competition field (Competitors__c / Others_Competitors_Please_specify__c / Next_Step__c) and in no verbatim buyer quote — it is unanchored and was likely inferred to fill a shortlist (offending: 'Focal Point (Anders Liljevic)')
